examples.title: Cyclic Code Encoder
examples.description: Systematic encoding of message using generator polynomial
examples.code:
FUNCTION EncodeCyclic(message[], n, k, g[])
// n = codeword length, k = message length
// g[] = coefficients of generator polynomial (degree n-k)
BEGIN
 r = n - k // number of parity bits
 
 // Step 1: Multiply message by x^r (shift left by r)
 temp[] = [0]*n
 FOR i FROM 0 TO k-1 DO
 temp[i+r] = message[i]
 END FOR
 
 // Step 2: Compute parity: remainder = temp mod g
 remainder[] = PolynomialDivision(temp, g)
 
 // Step 3: Append parity to shifted message
 codeword[] = temp
 FOR i FROM 0 TO r-1 DO
 codeword[n-r+i] = remainder[i]
 END FOR
 
 RETURN codeword
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION PolynomialDivision(dividend[], divisor[])
BEGIN
 deg_div = degree(divisor)
 temp[] = dividend
 
 WHILE degree(temp) >= deg_div DO
 factor = temp[degree(temp)] / divisor[deg_div]
 shift = degree(temp) - deg_div
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO deg_div DO
 temp[i+shift] = temp[i+shift] XOR (divisor[i] * factor)
 END FOR
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN temp // remainder
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This algorithm performs systematic encoding. Message is shifted by r positions, then remainder when divided by g(x) gives parity bits. XOR used instead of subtraction in GF(2).

examples.title: Syndrome Computation
examples.description: Compute syndrome polynomial for received word
examples.explanation: Syndrome computation divides received polynomial by parity check polynomial h(x). Zero syndrome means no error detected. This simulates the LFSR syndrome circuit behavior.

examples.title: Cyclic Shift Operation
examples.description: Perform cyclic (circular) left shift on codeword
examples.code:
FUNCTION CyclicShift(codeword[], n, shift)
// Shift left by 'shift' positions cyclically
BEGIN
 result[] = [0]*n
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO n-1 DO
 // New position wraps around using modulo
 new_pos = (i + shift) MOD n
 result[new_pos] = codeword[i]
 END FOR
 
 RETURN result
END FUNCTION

// Example: shift 1011 by 1 gives 0111
// Original: c0 c1 c2 c3
// Shifted 1: c3 c0 c1 c2
// Example: 1 0 1 1 -> 1 1 0 1

FUNCTION VerifyCyclicProperty(G[], n, k)
// Verify all cyclic shifts of each codeword are valid
BEGIN
 FOR each row IN G[] DO
 codeword = row
 FOR shift FROM 1 TO n-1 DO
 shifted = CyclicShift(codeword, n, shift)
 // Verify shifted is in code space (check if H×shifted = 0)
 IF NOT IsCodeword(shifted) THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Cyclic shift moves each bit to a new position with wraparound. Property: if c is valid codeword, all n cyclic shifts of c are also valid codewords. Demonstrates fundamental cyclic code property.

examples.title: Generator Matrix Construction
examples.description: Build generator matrix from generator polynomial for cyclic code
examples.explanation: Generator matrix constructed by taking cyclic shifts of generator polynomial. Each row represents one basis codeword. Rows form k-dimensional subspace of all valid codewords.
